reasoning: The infectious agent for Kawasaki disease remains unknown (as noted on the Wikipedia article itself), which makes developing a targeted vaccine virtually impossible at this stage. Even if the pathogen were to be discovered tomorrow, the typical vaccine development pipeline (including preclinical research, safety testing, Phase 1-3 clinical trials, and regulatory approval) typically takes 10+ years. Furthermore, Kawasaki disease is relatively rare, meaning a vaccine would not receive the emergency, accelerated funding seen during acute pandemics. Web searches verify that current medical literature discussing "Kawasaki disease" and "vaccines" is exclusively focused on whether other routine childhood vaccines could be potential triggers for the syndrome, or on how well existing vaccines work following standard immunoglobulin treatments for the disease. There are no ongoing vaccine development programs for Kawasaki disease itself. Therefore, the chance of a vaccine being successfully developed and documented on the specific Wikipedia page by the resolution dates is extremely minimal. I will provide a baseline 5% probability for the short and medium-term horizons (to respect the minimum allowed bounds), slowly increasing to 9% at the 10-year horizon to account for increasing uncertainty and possible (though highly improbable) scientific breakthroughs over a decade.
